title: Treat skill security audit as a moving target, not a static checklist
description: Add a SKILL_AUDIT_LAST_REVIEWED timestamp to key config and a staleness check in the self_evolve task. When the audit criteria haven't been manually reviewed in N days (suggested: 30), self_evolve logs a warning and surfaces it to the weekly reflection. The current soul_manager.audit_skill() checks for fixed patterns (base64, eval, curl-pipe-to-shell, etc.) but these were calibrated against a past threat landscape. As AI-assisted attack sophistication increases rapidly, static pattern lists go stale without any signal.
rationale: The reading note's core insight is that AI cyber capability is a slope (1.7 → 9.8 attack steps in 18 months), not a benchmark — defenses designed against last month's capabilities are already behind. Mira's skill audit is exactly this kind of static defense: it was written once, checks for known-bad patterns, and has no mechanism to signal when it might be outdated. Adding a staleness signal doesn't change the audit logic, but it changes the meta-posture from 'this is solved' to 'this needs periodic recalibration.'
diff_description: In config.py: add SKILL_AUDIT_LAST_REVIEWED = '2026-03-29' (ISO date string) and SKILL_AUDIT_REVIEW_INTERVAL_DAYS = 30. In soul_manager.py: add a function check_audit_staleness() that reads SKILL_AUDIT_LAST_REVIEWED, computes days since last review, and returns a warning string if over the interval. In the self_evolve agent or the reflect pipeline: call check_audit_staleness() and include the warning in the reflection output if triggered, prompting the user to re-examine audit_skill() patterns against current threat vectors.
source_note: 2026-03-29_网络攻击步骤数趋势不是-benchmark是斜率.md
